regions.name: Andalusia
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring (March-May) or fall for warm days without the scorching summer heat.

regions.name: Catalonia
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Late spring and early autumn offer pleasant weather for city and coastal exploring.

regions.name: Madrid
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Spring and fall provide mild temperatures ideal for sightseeing.

regions.name: Valencian Community
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Spring for Las Fallas festival or fall for beach weather without summer crowds.

regions.name: Basque Country
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Summer months offer the best weather for exploring the coast and cities.

regions.name: Galicia
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Summer is best for pleasant weather along the Atlantic coast and pilgrimage routes.

regions.name: Castile and León
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Late spring through early autumn is ideal for exploring historic towns.

regions.name: Castilla-La Mancha
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Spring and fall avoid the extreme summer heat of the plateau.

regions.name: Aragon
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Summer for Pyrenean hiking, winter for skiing in the mountains.

regions.name: Canary Islands
regions.capital: Las Palmas de Gran Canaria / Santa Cruz de Tenerife
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Year-round destination thanks to a mild subtropical climate, best in winter for northern escapees.

regions.name: Balearic Islands
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Late spring to early autumn is best for beaches and nightlife.

regions.name: Asturias
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Summer offers the best weather for hiking the green coastal mountains.

regions.name: Cantabria
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Summer months bring the mildest weather to this green northern coast.

regions.name: Navarre
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Early July for the Running of the Bulls, or spring/fall for quieter visits.

regions.name: La Rioja
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Fall harvest season (September-October) is ideal for wine touring.

regions.name: Extremadura
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Spring and autumn avoid the intense summer heat of this inland region.

regions.name: Region of Murcia
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Spring and fall offer warm days for beaches without peak summer heat.

regions.name: Ceuta and Melilla
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Spring and autumn offer comfortable temperatures for exploring these North African enclaves.

details.intro: Spain is a sun-drenched country of passionate culture, world-class art, stunning beaches, and vibrant cities, where flamenco rhythms, tapas culture, and centuries of history blend seamlessly. From the Pyrenees to Mediterranean coastlines and Moorish palaces to avant-garde architecture, Spain offers dramatic contrasts within a few hours' travel. Whether you're seeking cosmopolitan energy, rural charm, or coastal escape, Spain delivers across its 18 distinct regions.
details.gettingThere: International flights arrive at major hubs including Madrid-Barajas, Barcelona-El Prat, and Málaga; smaller regional airports serve other cities. Spain's extensive rail network (Renfe, AVE high-speed trains) connects major cities efficiently; buses are cheaper but slower. Renting a car gives flexibility for regional exploration, though cities are best navigated on foot or public transport.
details.daysNeeded: 10-14 days allows a genuine multi-region experience hitting major highlights; 7 days works for a focused coastal or city-based trip. Fewer than 5 days risks feeling rushed, especially with the distances between Spain's diverse regions.
details.bestTime: May-June and September-October offer warm weather, manageable crowds, and comfortable conditions across most regions; July-August is peak heat and tourism. Winter is mild in southern coastal areas but can be cold inland; spring wildflowers and autumn golden light reward flexible travelers.

itinerary.title: Madrid: Art and Urban Energy
itinerary.text: Arrive in Spain's capital and explore the Golden Triangle of Art—the Prado, Reina Sofía, and Thyssen museums—before wandering medieval La Latina and enjoying tapas in Plaza Mayor. Base yourself here to adjust to Spanish rhythms, which start late: dinner rarely begins before 9 PM.

itinerary.title: Madrid to Castile and León: Segovia
itinerary.text: Day trip or overnight to Segovia (90 minutes by train or car) to see the fairytale Alcázar castle, Roman aqueduct, and cathedral in this hillside medieval town. Return to Madrid or continue south.

itinerary.title: Madrid to Andalusia: Córdoba and Seville
itinerary.text: Travel by train or car south to Córdoba (4-5 hours) to tour the Mezquita, a stunning mosque-cathedral fusion, then continue to Seville (2 hours further) to experience its cathedral, Alcázar palace, and flamenco heartbeat.

itinerary.title: Seville: Flamenco and River Life
itinerary.text: Spend a full day in Seville's historic quarters—Barrio Santa Cruz's narrow streets, the Triana neighborhood across the Guadalquivir River—and catch an evening flamenco show. Walk the riverside at dusk when locals emerge for paseo (evening stroll).

itinerary.title: Andalusia: Granada and the Alhambra
itinerary.text: Travel to Granada (3 hours) and dedicate most of the day to the Alhambra, Islam's most complete palace complex with intricate tiles, courtyards, and Sierra Nevada views. Evening in the bohemian Albaicín neighborhood with Moorish tea shops and viewpoints.

itinerary.title: Granada: Coastal Escape to Costa Tropical
itinerary.text: Day trip to the beaches near Nerja (2 hours) or stay in Granada for museums and the cathedral. Alternatively, take a half-day bus to the white villages (pueblos blancos) of Andalusia—Ronda, Nerja, or Frigiliana—for dramatic cliffside vistas and authentic regional character.

itinerary.title: Fly or Train to Barcelona: Modernisme and Mediterranean
itinerary.text: Travel to Catalonia's capital (1-hour flight from Málaga, or 3+ hours by train from Granada). Explore Gaudí's Sagrada Familia, Park Güell's colorful mosaics, and Gothic Quarter's medieval streets before dinner in a beachside chiringuito (beach bar).

itinerary.title: Barcelona: Art, Architecture, and Food
itinerary.text: Visit the Picasso Museum, stroll Las Ramblas and Passeig de Gràcia's luxury shops, or climb Montjuïc for city views and the National Art Museum. End with seafood and vermouth in the atmospheric Born neighborhood or nearby beaches.

itinerary.title: Catalonia: Costa Brava or Montserrat
itinerary.text: Day trip to the jagged Costa Brava coastline and medieval towns like Tossa de Mar or Cadaqués (1-2 hours), or visit the dramatic Montserrat monastery and hiking area northwest of Barcelona. Both offer escape from city density while remaining close.

itinerary.title: Optional: Balearic Islands or Return
itinerary.text: If time allows, fly to Mallorca (1 hour from Barcelona) for beaches, mountain villages, and the cathedral of Palma, or return to Barcelona for final explorations. Alternatively, take the scenic train toward Valencia or the French border depending on interests.

faq.q: How many days do you need in Spain?
faq.a: 10-14 days allows a multi-region itinerary hitting Madrid, Andalusia, and Barcelona comfortably; 7 days works for one or two regions; fewer than 5 days is too rushed for meaningful experience of Spain's diversity.

Yes—Barcelona is essential for Gaudí's architecture (Sagrada Familia, Park Güell), world-class museums, Gothic medieval streets, and Mediterranean beaches; most first-time Spain visitors spend 2-3 days here.

faq.q: Should I rent a car in Spain?
faq.a: Only if exploring rural regions, small towns, or the countryside; in major cities (Madrid, Barcelona, Seville) public transport and walking suffice and parking is expensive and stressful. A car is valuable for Andalusia's white villages or Catalonia's coast.

faq.q: What is the best time to visit Spain?
faq.a: May-June and September-October are ideal—warm weather without peak summer heat or crowds; July-August is very hot and crowded; winter is mild in the south (Málaga, southern coast) but cold inland.

faq.q: Do I need to speak Spanish to travel in Spain?
faq.a: English is widely spoken in major cities, tourist areas, and by younger people, but learning basic Spanish phrases is helpful and appreciated, especially in smaller towns and rural regions where English is less common.
